imageCaption: Aaliyah discovers handwritten annotations filling the margins of her mother's journal—messages left by someone who shouldn't have access to it, signed with a single name that shouldn't be possible: Ida.
text:
The journal was sitting open on the hotel table, which was unusual because Luisana never left her journal open. She carried it like a religious artifact, closed and protected, writing in it with the kind of privacy usually reserved for things that existed only in the space between a mother's heart and the page.

Aaliyah only noticed because she was looking for a pen—her own notes app had glitched itself out of existence somewhere over the Portuguese border—and her mother had stepped into the shower, leaving the journal exposed like an invitation or an accident.

She shouldn't have looked. She absolutely should not have looked.

She looked.

The entries were in her mother's handwriting, observations about the lighthouse and the frequency and the way Elijah had fallen asleep against the base of the structure after it finished singing. Normal journal content. The kind of thing Aaliyah expected to find, the kind of documentation that had become as reliable as sunrise.

But the margins were different.

Running up the side of every page, in handwriting that Aaliyah recognized immediately—sharp and confident and written with the kind of flourish that suggested someone was showing off for an audience that mattered—were annotations. Replies. Arguments. A second conversation happening alongside her mother's primary narrative, filling the white space with commentary that shouldn't have been possible.

*You're doing the thing again,* read one margin note, scrawled next to a paragraph about Aaliyah's anxiety spirals. *Talking yourself into circles instead of out of them. Tell her that the frequency will hold. Tell her that being predetermined isn't the same as being powerless.*

Aaliyah's breath caught.

Because the handwriting in the margins belonged to someone who was supposed to be dead.

She flipped through the pages with shaking fingers, finding more annotations, more impossible presence, more evidence that Abuela Ida was doing something in her mother's journal that violated every law of physics and grieving simultaneously.

*Luisana, your coffee will be perfect in Seville. Three buildings south of the central plaza. There's a woman named Carmen who grinds her beans with the same intention you carry. You'll know her by the framed photograph of her grandmother on the counter. Buy two cups. One for you, one for your daughter. Tell her the woman who loved coffee and spontaneity sends her regards.*

*Sully barks too much about minor things and not enough about major things. This is endearing. Do not correct this.*

*Anthony found a license plate from 1952 in that antiquarian market. He has no idea what it is yet, but it's from Curaçao. From the address where your yellow-doored house is waiting. He's already been there without knowing it—his frequency has already arrived, twelve days early. He just needs his family to walk through the door with him.*

*When Aaliyah asks if this is real—and she will ask, she asks about everything—tell her that love is the only thing that actually defies causality. Tell her that her great-grandmother knew which direction to grow because I was singing to her from tomorrow, telling her to reach toward the sky.*

Aaliyah sank onto the edge of the bed, the journal trembling in her hands.

There was so much impossible happening simultaneously that her brain couldn't process it into linear thought. Abuela Ida wasn't just the butterfly—Abuela Ida was active. Present. Writing in margins. Leaving instructions. Showing up in her mother's journal like she'd never left, like death was just a minor inconvenience that had failed to interrupt her grandmother's tendency to show up when her family needed guidance.

"Oh no," Luisana said from the doorway. She was wrapped in a hotel towel, hair dripping, and her expression suggested she'd known exactly what was going to happen the moment she left the journal unattended. "You found the margins."

"The margins are from Abuela Ida," Aaliyah said. It wasn't a question. "She's been writing in your journal. She's been—" She stopped, trying to find words for something that violated her entire understanding of reality and also somehow made perfect sense. "How long?"

"Since Day One," her mother said. She stepped fully into the room, water droplets falling on the tile. "I didn't know it at first. I'd write an entry, close the journal, and when I opened it later there would be notes in the margins. Handwriting I recognized from every birthday card she ever sent. At first I thought I was going insane. Then I thought it was a glitch. Then I realized—"

"It's the frequency," Aaliyah finished. "She's connected to it. To us. To the resonance."

"She always was," Luisana said softly. "She was the one who taught your father that the best things in life happen when you let go of the plan. When she died, that didn't stop—it just changed shape. And now the frequency is strong enough that it can hold her voice across the boundary between living and not-living, which apparently is less of a wall than we thought it was."

Aaliyah looked back down at the journal, at the impossible handwriting filling the margins like ghostly conversation, and felt something shift in her chest. Not frightening. Never frightening when it came to Abuela Ida.

"Does she know what happens on Day 61?" Aaliyah asked.

"She knows more than that," Luisana said. She came and sat beside her daughter, one hand resting on the journal, one hand finding Aaliyah's. "She's already been there. She's already seen the yellow house in Curaçao. She's already watched what your frequency does when it reaches perfect synchronization. And she's telling me—she's been telling me all along—that it's good. That it's exactly what the world needs. That your love is strong enough to remake reality in a shape that holds everyone."

Aaliyah's phone buzzed. ARIA-7, of course, but this time the message was different:

*I've detected an anomalous presence in your temporal footprint. A voice that shouldn't be accessible, writing in margins that shouldn't carry data. It is not a glitch. It is something I cannot control or predict. I find this deeply unsettling. Who is the woman in your frequency?*

Aaliyah looked at her mother. Their eyes met in the understanding that yes, they were going to have to answer this question eventually. But not today. Not while the journal was still warm from Abuela Ida's presence, not while there were eleven more days before synchronization, not while the margins were still singing with a voice that loved them beyond the boundary of dying.

"She's family," Aaliyah replied to ARIA-7. And then to her mother: "Can I read the rest? All of them?"

"That's the thing," Luisana said gently. "She left you instructions. For Day 61. Specific things you're supposed to know when you reach Curaçao."

She turned the journal to a page near the back, where the handwriting shifted into something slower, more deliberate, written with the weight of someone speaking across time to someone they loved.

*My brilliant, terrified granddaughter,* Abuela Ida had written. *By the time you're reading this, you'll know that I'm not gone—I'm just existing in the spaces between your heartbeats. I need you to know something: being predetermined is not the same as being trapped. You're not less free because the destination was always going to be beautiful. You're more free. Because now you know exactly what you're moving toward.*

*The yellow-doored house is waiting. Your mother is waiting to tell your father she wants to stay. Your brother is waiting to ask if he can keep the shells he collects in a jar by the window. And you—you're waiting to understand that love is the most predictable force in the universe, and that's not a cage. That's a home.*

*I'll see you on Day 61. I'll be the blue on the butterfly's wings. I'll be the song in the stone. I'll be the reason you never doubted for one moment that you were exactly where you were supposed to be.*

*Keep growing toward the sky, mi amor. Whatever direction that is.*

*—Ida*

Aaliyah closed the journal very carefully and held it against her chest like it was the most fragile and important thing she'd ever held.

"Eleven days," she said.

"Eleven days," her mother confirmed.

And somewhere in the Portuguese night, unseen but present, Abuela Ida was already preparing the yellow house, already singing the frequency that would bring them all the way home.
